agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A new 'auto-split' feature is proposed that would automatically divide shipments exceeding 500 kg into multiple sub-shipments, each assigned to a different carrier, bypassing supervisor involvement. This must be evaluated against the established, production domain model governing Shipment-Carrier relationships.","bounded_context":"Shipment Management / Carrier Assignment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of goods to be transported, which must have exactly one assigned Carrier."},{"term":"Carrier","definition":"The entity responsible for transporting a Shipment."},{"term":"Sub-shipment","definition":"A proposed new concept: a child Shipment created by splitting a parent Shipment that exceeds a weight threshold."},{"term":"Carrier Assignment","definition":"The binding of exactly one Carrier to a Shipment. Once set, only a Supervisor can change it."},{"term":"Supervisor","definition":"A privileged role that is the only actor authorized to reassign a Carrier on a Shipment."},{"term":"Auto-split","definition":"Proposed automatic process that splits an overweight Shipment into multiple sub-shipments and assigns each to a different Carrier without Supervisor involvement."},{"term":"Dispatch","definition":"The point at which a Shipment is sent out; the system guarantees every dispatched Shipment has exactly one Carrier."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a data integrity and state consistency guarantee. A Shipment with zero or multiple Carriers is a corrupt state — accountability, tracking, and liability all depend on a single Carrier per Shipment.","testable_assertion":"For every Shipment in any non-draft state, COUNT(assigned_carriers) == 1."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, only a Supervisor can reassign it.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is an authorization invariant protecting operational integrity. Unauthorized reassignment could break chain-of-custody, insurance liability, and contractual obligations. Removing this guard corrupts the trust model.","testable_assertion":"Any Carrier reassignment event on a Shipment must have an actor with the Supervisor role; otherwise the operation is rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"At the time of dispatch, every Shipment must have exactly one Carrier assigned.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Dispatch without a single accountable Carrier is a physical and legal impossibility in logistics — someone must be responsible for the goods in transit.","testable_assertion":"The dispatch operation must fail if the Shipment does not have exactly one assigned Carrier."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A shipment weighs exactly 500 kg — does it split or not?","expected_behavior":"Boundary must be clearly defined (> 500 vs >= 500). Domain ambiguity exists."},{"scenario":"A shipment is split after a Carrier has already been assigned to the original.","expected_behavior":"The original Carrier assignment is effectively removed or altered. This constitutes a reassignment without Supervisor involvement, violating BR-2."},{"scenario":"A sub-shipment itself exceeds 500 kg after splitting.","expected_behavior":"Recursive splitting rules are undefined. Could lead to unbounded subdivision."},{"scenario":"A shipment is split mid-transit or after dispatch.","expected_behavior":"Splitting a dispatched shipment would retroactively violate BR-3 for the original shipment and require new dispatch events for sub-shipments."},{"scenario":"One sub-shipment's carrier fails pickup — who is accountable for the parent shipment?","expected_behavior":"Accountability model is undefined. The one-Carrier-per-Shipment invariant exists precisely to prevent ambiguous accountability."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Incorrect splitting logic or ambiguous carrier accountability could lead to lost goods, insurance claim disputes, and contractual liability gaps. Each sub-shipment also incurs separate carrier costs, potentially increasing total shipping expense.","operational":"Introduces a new 'sub-shipment' aggregate that does not exist in the current domain model. Every downstream system (tracking, billing, dispatch, proof-of-delivery) must be updated to understand parent-child shipment relationships. This is not a feature — it is a domain model evolution.","user":"Customers currently see one shipment with one carrier. Splitting introduces multiple tracking numbers, multiple delivery windows, and partial delivery scenarios. Customer experience must be redesigned.","risk":"HIGH. The feature as stated directly contradicts two production invariants. Shipping it as described would corrupt the domain model and break every system that depends on the one-Carrier-per-Shipment guarantee.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Operations / Dispatch","impact":"Must handle a new sub-shipment lifecycle, partial dispatches, and multi-carrier coordination for a single customer order."},{"group":"Supervisors","impact":"Their exclusive authority over carrier reassignment is bypassed. This undermines the control structure that exists for liability and operational safety reasons."},{"group":"Carriers","impact":"Now responsible for a portion of a shipment rather than a whole one. Contractual terms, SLAs, and liability boundaries must be renegotiated."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Receive partial deliveries, multiple tracking numbers, and potentially conflicting ETAs. Support burden increases."},{"group":"Finance / Billing","impact":"Invoicing must handle parent-child shipment cost allocation. Revenue recognition may change if partial shipments are delivered across billing periods."},{"group":"Legal / Compliance","impact":"Chain-of-custody documentation, insurance coverage, and liability assignment all assume one carrier per shipment. Splitting without legal review creates exposure."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The specification contains a direct contradiction with two established production invariants (one-Carrier-per-Shipment and Supervisor-only reassignment). The feature as described cannot be implemented without violating the existing domain model. The contradiction is unambiguous and structural, not a matter of interpretation. Confidence is low because the spec cannot be accepted as-is — it needs fundamental redesign.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction between auto-split and BR-1/BR-2 is clear and unambiguous","The feature introduces a new aggregate (sub-shipment) that does not exist in the current model","Supervisor authority is explicitly bypassed"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the product team intends sub-shipments to be first-class Shipments or a new entity type","Whether the 500 kg threshold is a hard business constraint or a configurable policy","What the intended accountability model is when multiple carriers handle parts of one logical shipment"],"assumptions":["The existing domain model (BR-1, BR-2, BR-3) is correctly stated and actively enforced in production","Sub-shipments would need to follow the same lifecycle as Shipments unless a new aggregate is explicitly defined"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature directly contradicts two production invariants: (1) BR-1 requires exactly one Carrier per Shipment — splitting assigns multiple carriers to what is logically one shipment. (2) BR-2 requires Supervisor involvement for any carrier reassignment — auto-split explicitly bypasses Supervisor authority. These are not edge cases; they are fundamental conflicts at the core of the domain model.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations","Supervisors","Carriers","Customers","Legal/Compliance"],"options":["A: Reject the feature as incompatible with the current domain model.","B: Introduce a new 'Composite Shipment' aggregate that contains multiple child Shipments, each preserving the one-Carrier invariant. The parent is not a Shipment — it is a new domain concept (e.g., 'Shipment Group' or 'Freight Order'). Splitting creates new Shipments, not sub-shipments. Supervisor approval is required for the split operation.","C: Same as B, but allow auto-split without Supervisor approval by treating it as an initial assignment (not a reassignment) — the original Shipment is cancelled and new ones are created. This still requires Supervisor sign-off on the cancellation per BR-2.","D: Relax BR-2 to allow system-initiated reassignment for split scenarios specifically, with an audit trail. This is a deliberate policy change, not a bug."],"recommendation":"Option B. Introduce a 'Shipment Group' or 'Freight Order' aggregate that owns multiple Shipments. Each child Shipment independently satisfies BR-1 (one Carrier). The split operation should require Supervisor approval to honor BR-2. The 500 kg threshold can trigger a recommendation, but the actual split must be authorized. This preserves all invariants while enabling the business capability."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Auto-split assigns multiple carriers to a single logical shipment","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier"},{"violation":"Auto-split bypasses Supervisor authority for carrier assignment changes","business_rule_broken":"BR-2: Only a Supervisor can reassign a Carrier on a Shipment"},{"violation":"Splitting a shipment after carrier assignment constitutes an unauthorized reassignment","business_rule_broken":"BR-2: Only a Supervisor can reassign a Carrier on a Shipment"}]}
